topic: My basement gas meter spins for forty minutes between three and four in the morning every night even with the main shutoff valve closed and the utility company says my usage matches a household of fourteen people
title: My Gas Meter Spins for Forty Minutes Every Night With the Main Valve Shut. The Utility Says I Use Enough for Fourteen People. | True Scary Story
description_intro: My winter gas bill tripled last fall. The utility sent a technician to inspect the line. He told me my consumption matched the usage of a household of fourteen people. There are three people living in my house. We sat together in the basement at three in the morning and watched the meter. The main shutoff valve was closed with a wrench. The dial spun steadily for forty minutes.
description:
My winter gas bill tripled last fall. The utility sent a technician to inspect the line. He told me my consumption matched the usage of a household of fourteen people. There are three people living in my house. We sat together in the basement at three in the morning and watched the meter. The main shutoff valve was closed with a wrench. The dial spun steadily for forty minutes.

Three plumbers tested the lines with manometers and soap solution and found nothing. The utility's gas sniffer found nothing. A battery camera I set up showed the same pattern every night. The dial began turning at three in the morning and stopped at three forty. Outside that window it did not move. The pilot lights were dark. The water heater was cold. I went to the county building and pulled the pre nineteen seventy utility records for my address.

There was a second gas service line at this house. It was marked abandoned in nineteen seventy one. It ran into a small chamber that had been sealed off behind a stud wall in nineteen seventy three. There is a detail about what was inside that chamber, and what the contractor told me the next morning, that I have only told my brother. Watch to the end.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: My gas bill tripled last winter. The first month I assumed it was the cold. The second month I called the utility company and asked them to send a technician. He came on a Wednesday afternoon and walked through every appliance in the house. He went down to the basement and stood in front of the meter for a long time. When he came back upstairs he told me my consumption matched the usage of a household of fourteen people. There are three people in my house. He suggested we sit together at three in the morning and watch the meter. I agreed. We turned every appliance off. We closed the main shutoff valve at the wall with a wrench. We sat on the basement floor in folding chairs. The dial spun for forty minutes.

sections.narration: Our house is a one story brick bungalow built in nineteen fifty six on a quiet street in an older neighborhood. We bought it in two thousand twenty two from the estate of a woman who had lived there alone for forty years. The basement is finished on the front half and unfinished on the back half. The back half is where the mechanical equipment sits. The water heater is in the back left corner against the foundation wall. The furnace is in the back right corner. The gas meter is mounted on the foundation wall directly between them, about three feet off the concrete floor. There is a single bare bulb hanging from a ceiling joist above the meter. The main shutoff valve sits on the supply pipe a few inches above the meter housing. The basement stairs come down on the front side.

sections.narration: There are three of us living in the house. My wife and I and our eight year old daughter. My wife works from home three days a week. Our daughter is in second grade at the elementary school four blocks away. I commute to an office twenty miles east. We cook dinner most nights on a gas range. The water heater is gas. The furnace is gas. The clothes dryer is electric. Our usage had been steady and predictable for the three years we had owned the house. I check the meter myself every quarter when I balance the household budget against the utility statements. The numbers had always matched the bills within a few therms. There had never been a discrepancy worth a phone call. Last fall the bills started arriving with totals I did not recognize. The discrepancy was not small.

sections.narration: I went online and pulled the full billing history for the account. The utility kept records going back five years. October was normal. November was triple the previous November. December was triple again. January was four times the previous January. February dropped back to almost normal. March was normal. The pattern repeated the prior winter. Triple in November. Triple in December. Quadruple in January. Almost normal in February. The winter before that was the same. Three winters in a row. I had not noticed because the bills had risen gradually within each season and our autopay had absorbed the totals without flagging anything. The fall of two thousand twenty two had been our first months in the house and we had blamed the high bills on settling in. The same explanation had carried us through the next two winters without question.

sections.narration: My first assumption was a leak in the supply lines somewhere inside the walls. I called three independent plumbers over the course of two weeks. The first plumber tested every fitting in the basement with a manometer and a bottle of soap solution. He found nothing. The second plumber pulled drywall in the laundry room and checked the lines feeding the dryer and the water heater. He found nothing. The third plumber traced the supply from the meter to every appliance with a handheld combustible gas detector. He found nothing. The utility company sent their own technician out a second time with a more sensitive gas sniffer and a thermal camera. He walked the entire house top to bottom. He found nothing. Every line in the house was sealed and accounted for. There was no leak anywhere in the system to explain the bills.

sections.narration: I bought a small battery powered security camera and mounted it on a shelf across from the meter. The lens was aimed directly at the dial. I set it to record continuously and let it run for one full week. On Sunday morning I sat at the kitchen table and scrubbed through every night of footage frame by frame. The dial was motionless from the moment I went to bed until exactly three in the morning. At three in the morning the dial began to spin. It spun steadily for forty minutes. At three forty in the morning the dial stopped. It stayed motionless until I woke up. The same window every night. Three in the morning to three forty in the morning. Seven nights in a row. The numerical reading on the face of the meter rose by the same amount each night.

sections.narration: I called the utility company and asked them to send a technician out on a Saturday night. They agreed. He arrived at two thirty in the morning with a clipboard and a small flashlight. We went down to the basement together. I closed the main shutoff valve at the wall with a pipe wrench. He confirmed the valve was physically closed by trying to turn it himself. We sat on the floor in folding chairs and waited. At three in the morning exactly the dial began to spin. We both watched it. We could not hear a whoosh of gas anywhere in the line. We could not feel flow at any of the appliances upstairs. The pilot light on the water heater was dark. The water heater itself was cold to the touch. The furnace was silent. The dial kept spinning steadily.

sections.narration: The technician left after sunrise without an explanation. He wrote unable to determine source on his clipboard and signed it. On Monday morning I drove to the county building downtown and went up to the records department on the third floor. I asked the clerk for the pre nineteen seventy utility drawings for my address. She brought up a folder from a back archive. Inside were the original gas service drawings for the house from nineteen fifty six. There were also revisions from nineteen seventy one and nineteen seventy three. The drawings showed that the house had originally been built with two gas service lines instead of one. The second line had been marked abandoned in nineteen seventy one. The drawings showed the second line ran from the basement into the original coal cellar. The coal cellar had been sealed off behind a stud wall in nineteen seventy three.

sections.narration: I went home and waited until Sunday afternoon when my wife and daughter were at her mother's house. I pried off the drywall of the sealed cellar wall in the back of the basement with a flat bar. Behind it was a stud wall built from old rough cut lumber. I pulled the studs out one at a time. Behind that was a small chamber about the size of a closet with a low concrete ceiling. Inside the chamber there was a brass fitting connected to the abandoned line at the back wall. The fitting was screwed to a long black rubber tube. The tube disappeared down through a hole drilled cleanly into the concrete floor. The tube was warm to the touch. From the hole came a sound. A low and constant exhale, then an inhale. A breath. Slow and steady. Patient.

sections.narration: I called the utility company on Monday morning and described what I had found. The representative pulled the service records for the address. She told me the abandoned line had been physically severed at the street in nineteen seventy one. The utility had no record of a brass fitting anywhere on the property. She told me to seal the cellar back up and leave it alone. I called a contractor that afternoon and scheduled a concrete pour to fill the entire chamber on Tuesday morning. Monday night the meter spun for ninety minutes instead of forty. Tuesday morning at six the contractor's truck was already parked in the driveway when I opened the front door. He stepped out and shook my hand. He told me he had been there since overnight. He had already poured the cellar. He said I had let him in at three.
